title: Lincoln's Friend Leonard Swett
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in his home in Sewanee, Tennessee, in June 2013, Scott Bates opens with his Evanston childhood near Lake Michigan, his 1923 birth into a family of Chicago lawyers descended from his great-uncle Leonard Swett, a friend of Abraham Lincoln, and his delight that dry Evanston drove him to Carleton and dry Tennessee ran with the moonshine that once filled Al Capone's Chicago trucks.

part: A French Interpreter in the War
title: Lying on Omaha Beach
summary: Drafted in 1942 and trained in French through the Army's ASTP program at the University of Chicago, Bates describes landing under fire at Omaha Beach with no air cover, waking in a field hospital with the beach sand still in his hair, the 1,300 bombers that reduced Saint-Lo to rubble, and the drive to Avranches with Patton's Third Army as he became a French interpreter expecting to help run occupied Germany.

title: The Horror Houses of Romilly
summary: Stationed near Romilly for a winter while Eisenhower kept his unit out of the Battle of the Bulge to run Germany, Bates recounts supervising the legal military brothels, the stark contrast between the two regulated houses serving white troops and the many unsupervised illegal houses left to Black troops, and the disease that followed.

title: The Worst Was Not D-Day
summary: Bates describes Avranches as the key point between Brittany and Normandy, first glimpsing Mont-Saint-Michel across the bay, enduring three weeks of Hitler's counterattack and dive-bombing he calls worse than D-Day, and sheltering in a convent with a sergeant, two PFCs, and a French girl whose head had been shaved for sleeping with Germans while the enemy targeted the bridge below the town.

title: Walking the MP Beat in French
topic: Race in the Army and Back Home
summary: Returning to race, Bates describes how the Army separated Black and white troops to the town's segregated brothels after fights with white Southern soldiers, his nights on military police patrol with a Black soldier from Lincoln College discussing seventeenth-century English literature, and the Black classmates and Howard Street drinking of his Chicago boyhood that had made integration familiar.

title: A One-Year Job That Lasted a Lifetime
summary: Bates traces his path back to Carleton, a doctorate at the University of Wisconsin on the GI Bill, and a one-year French post at Sewanee, the University of the South, a white boys' college of 400 in a Tennessee town of 400, recalling a boyhood stay in Orangeburg, South Carolina and a Black maid in Evanston, and how the job became permanent.

title: Seminaries Across the Street
summary: Serving on the board of trustees of the heavily Episcopal, roughly ninety percent segregationist University of the South, Bates describes the fight to desegregate after closing Black Episcopal seminaries had to send would-be priests somewhere, naming Seabury-Western and Garrett seminaries in Evanston and the theologian Rosemary Radford Ruether.

title: From the Fellowship to the War Resisters
summary: Bates recounts the Fellowship of Reconciliation's anti-war work, reading the pacifist theologian John Swomley, then moving to the War Resisters League in Greenwich Village, and an activist he calls John Mac whose daily broadcasts tracked anti-war and socialist action across America and Egypt.

title: A Calendar of Anti-War Poems
summary: He tells of a long-distance call from Bayard Rustin asking to use the anti-war poetry he had collected since high school, the resulting War Resisters League calendars that ran a poem each year, and the published collection of poems of war resistance that sold thirteen thousand copies and was preached from pulpits across the country.

title: A Poem a Week, Free to All
summary: Bates describes running a poem a week in the local Sewanee paper, first typed and published by his wife and later carried by the Sewanee Mountain Messenger, choosing out-of-copyright anti-war and holiday verse handed out free so no one could pursue him for copyright.

title: Hitched to the Sewanee Review
summary: He describes Sewanee as a liberal arts school open to New Yorker and progressive poems, and an English department that, though housing many Unitarians and former Episcopal priests, was tied to the Sewanee Review, the oldest literary magazine in the country, where his own poems occasionally appeared.

title: V. Gene Robinson and the First Black Graduate
summary: Bates recalls graduate V. Gene Robinson, the first openly gay Episcopal bishop, the campus gay-straight alliance, and the long board fights to admit women in 1969 and Black students through the decade, including a top honors student from Alabama who became a federal judge as the first Black graduate, as priests and alumni left the desegregating church.

title: A Union School in the Hills
summary: Asked for background, Bates explains that Highlander Folk School was the union organizing school of the South, that he had never heard of it before arriving and found in it his life's dedication, and points to the University of North Carolina account of how the unions founded it on the social gospel as far back as 1905.

title: Myles Horton at Union Seminary
summary: He sketches Myles Horton, a sharecropper's son from East Tennessee who rose from a country college to Union Theological Seminary in New York to study under Reinhold Niebuhr, alongside a radical Christian educator Bates struggles to name, the two figures who most shaped Horton's thinking.

title: From Jane Addams to a Danish Model
summary: Bates recounts how Niebuhr sent Horton to Jane Addams in Chicago and to study the Danish folk schools, and how Horton combined them with a retired Memphis woman educator's mountain home to start a folk school in Grundy County, one of the poorest counties in the country, near Tracy City and Monteagle.

title: Three in the Morning with a Bouncer
summary: He describes Grundy County's moonshine and his doctor, a 350-pound man who at thirteen had been a nightclub bouncer, telling stories at three in the morning during Bates's hospital stay, and frames the wooded mountain with its deer and panthers as a Shangri-La for a Michigan-raised bird watcher.

title: Victor Hugo Against the Gun
summary: Bates connects his leftist French literature teaching, including a Victor Hugo poem against gun control he still places in local papers, to his War Resisters League stance and the small anti-Vietnam movement he led among his students, naming the poet Richard Tillinghast, a good student who later turned conservative.

title: Kids Running the Discussion
summary: He recalls his first Highlander meeting, where white University of Chattanooga and Knoxville students working to integrate the University of Tennessee met Black students from Talladega and ran the whole discussion themselves while Myles Horton stayed out of it, and he describes Horton's teasing, martini-fueled, storytelling style that Studs Terkel prized.

title: Greensboro and Nashville Come Together
summary: Bates describes the Highlander meeting where the Greensboro and Nashville sit-in students came together and Horton told them they had to do it themselves, then recalls a later three-thousand-person march down State Street in Chicago after the Cicero riots, shouting down Mayor Daley at Grant Park where Obama would later speak.

part: The NAACP Chapter and the Trial
title: We'll Take It, But Not Publicly
summary: He recounts a national NAACP meeting where Roger Wilkins told him the organization would take what SNCC and the Southern groups offered but would not recognize them publicly, and how he started a local NAACP chapter under Septima Clark, Highlander's education director, against the grain of a cautious Black establishment.

title: Half the Church Walked Out
summary: Bates describes Septima Clark desegregating the Sewanee chapel, two Black women walking to the front while half the congregation rose and left for good, and the school-desegregation court case in Franklin County where Avon Williams Jr. returned every week for two years before an armed courtroom the police would not let erupt.

title: License Plates Sent to the FBI
topic: The John Birch Society and Surveillance
summary: He recounts Southern stalling after the Supreme Court ruling, a millionaire student organizing the John Birch Society around Sewanee, a restaurant owner taking down Highlander visitors' license plates for the FBI, and a sit-in at Tubby's beer house where the agents investigated the activists rather than the segregationists.

title: The Communist Training School Billboard
summary: Bates tells of bringing in the Nashville lawyers Avon Williams and Looby for the desegregation case, the Sewanee hero Mrs. Fowler who ran the NAACP chapter, and the 1957 Highlander photograph of Martin Luther King and Rosa Parks turned into billboards branding the school a Communist training center, with Bates himself visible against the wall.

title: A Stone House Burned by the Parish
summary: He describes how the Highlander land was sold off cheap and gentrified into a summer suburb after the school was run out, the cemetery across the road where the Horton family and May Justice are buried, the highway marker finally raised, and Myles Horton's mountain-stone house burned down by a parish whose minister begged God's pardon for it.

title: The Red Dean and Seditious Books
topic: Highlander's Place and a Long Feud
summary: Bates reflects that Highlander went unmentioned for years until the Obama era restored its history, recounts his twenty-year campus feud with an anti-gay, anti-Black dean called the Red Dean, and ties his French teaching to seditious banned books like the Marquis de Sade kept on reserve in the library.

title: A Poem Nobody Will Publish
topic: Banned French Verse and His Library
summary: He describes the socialist, Christian-socialist radicalism behind a French anti-gun poem he says The Nation and others refuse to print, and a passage from a writer answering Sade that remains banned in France yet circulates in underground book series and Paris bookstores, then offers to show the interviewers his cabin library.

title: Places That Trained and Sent Them On
topic: Aldon Morris and the Social Gospel
summary: Bates engages the sociologist Aldon Morris, whose book calls Highlander and Hull House movement halfway houses that trained and inspired organizers, distinguishes him from the Episcopal activist John B. Morris, and explains why the social-gospel Horton distrusted official bodies like the NAACP and went to the grassroots.

part: You Gotta Do It Yourself, Again
title: Thoreau Fetched From Sewanee
summary: Returning to the Highlander meeting that preceded the Shaw University gathering where SNCC was founded, Bates recalls his student Richard Tillinghast driving back to Sewanee to fetch Thoreau's Civil Disobedience for John Lewis, Stokely Carmichael, and the others, Horton's line that they had to do it themselves because the whites would not stick with them, and the no-guns rule that turned away armed Chicago Black Panthers.

title: A Busload to Johns Island
topic: Esau Jenkins and the Charleston Coast
summary: Bates describes a Knoxville Black district where he, Esau Jenkins, and Andrew Young stayed at a little church, a bus trip taking Black children from Johns Island to the white-only resort islands off Charleston where white youths jeered and Septima Clark chided their manners, staying with Guy Carawan near Charleston, a closely guarded Martin Luther King lecture, and an all-day beach meeting circled by police cars.

title: Still Singing the Same Songs
topic: A Lifetime on the Highlander Board
summary: Bates closes on his time on the Highlander board since 1958 and as president through the 1960s, the field meetings in Jackson and Memphis he reported back, how he ended the written minutes so members spoke truth at oral meetings, and his sense that it remains the same freedom movement, still singing the same songs, now joining gay and Black action, with a Southern Echo leader who opens every meeting with a song.
